title: Selection Sort

glossary.definition: Moving an element far away from its original position directly, jumping over other items.
The amount of extra work the computer has to do just to save or move data in memory.
An organizing method that works by repeatedly asking 'is A bigger than B?'

hook.scenario: You are writing firmware for an IoT sensor that stores calibration data on Flash memory. Flash cells physically degrade and eventually fail after a finite number of write operations.
hook.question: How do you sort the calibration data while performing the absolute minimum number of memory writes?

why_it_exists.problem: Some storage media (EEPROM, NAND Flash) have asymmetric read/write costs. Reads are virtually free, but writes physically degrade the hardware. Write-heavy sorting algorithms can literally destroy the memory.
why_it_exists.naive_solution: Use Bubble Sort or Insertion Sort, which can perform up to O(N^2) swaps or shifts, rapidly consuming the hardware's limited write endurance.
why_it_exists.better_idea: Scan the entire unsorted portion to find the minimum element, then perform exactly one swap to place it. This trades O(N^2) comparisons (cheap reads) for a maximum of N-1 swaps (expensive writes).

mental_model.intuition: Imagine lining up students by height. Instead of shuffling everyone around, you scan the whole unsorted line with your eyes (reads), find the shortest person, and walk them directly to the front (one swap). Repeat for the remaining line.
mental_model.description: The array is divided into a sorted left partition and an unsorted right partition. In each pass, the algorithm scans the entire unsorted partition to locate the absolute minimum element. It then swaps this minimum with the first element of the unsorted partition, growing the sorted partition by one.
mental_model.repeated_decision: Is `arr[j] < arr[min_index]`? If yes, update `min_index = j`. After the inner loop, swap `arr[i]` with `arr[min_index]`.

invariants.inv-min: Each pass scans the unsorted suffix to find its smallest element.
invariants.inv-prefix: arr[0..i-1] holds the i smallest elements, already in final order.
invariants.inv-sorted: When the sorted prefix covers the array, it's sorted — in ≤ N-1 swaps.

explanation:
Selection Sort always performs exactly N*(N-1)/2 comparisons, regardless of the input data. Whether the array is already sorted, reverse-sorted, or random, the algorithm executes the same number of comparison operations. It is completely non-adaptive.

However, it performs at most N-1 swaps. Each pass through the inner loop identifies the minimum of the unsorted portion and executes exactly one swap to place it. This makes Selection Sort the optimal choice when writes are expensive.

The loop invariant is: at the start of the i-th iteration, the subarray `arr[0..i-1]` contains the i smallest elements of the original array, in their final sorted positions. Once an element is placed, it is never touched again.

Critically, Selection Sort is NOT stable. The long-distance swap can destroy relative order. Example: `[3A, 3B, 1]`. Finding min=1, we swap it with 3A: `[1, 3B, 3A]`. The relative order of the two 3s has been reversed.

key_points.title: Minimum Writes
key_points.detail: At most N-1 swaps. The lowest write overhead of any comparison-based sort.

key_points.title: Non-Adaptive
key_points.detail: Always O(N^2) comparisons regardless of input order. It has no mechanism to detect pre-existing sortedness.

key_points.title: Unstable
key_points.detail: Long-distance swaps destroy the relative order of equal elements.

pattern.name: Global Minimum Search
recognition_cues: The problem explicitly requires minimizing memory writes.
You need to find the K smallest/largest elements without fully sorting.

failure_signals: You need a stable sort for multi-key ordering. Selection Sort will corrupt secondary key ordering.
You are using Selection Sort on a nearly-sorted array. It will still perform O(N^2) comparisons because it cannot detect existing order.

engineering_examples.title: Flash Memory / EEPROM Firmware
engineering_examples.problem: Sorting data on storage with limited Program/Erase cycles.
engineering_examples.why_this_algorithm: It minimizes writes to N-1, extending the physical lifespan of the hardware.

engineering_examples.title: K-th Order Statistics
engineering_examples.problem: Finding the top K highest-priority items in a scheduler.
engineering_examples.why_this_algorithm: Run exactly K passes of Selection Sort to extract the K smallest/largest elements in O(K*N) time without wasting effort sorting the rest.

when_not_to_use.reason: Selection Sort's long-distance swaps destroy relative ordering of equal elements. Use Insertion Sort or Merge Sort instead.
Selection Sort is non-adaptive and will always perform O(N^2) comparisons. Insertion Sort handles nearly-sorted data in O(N).

common_mistakes.title: Confusing with Insertion Sort
common_mistakes.explanation: Selection Sort finds the minimum and places it. Insertion Sort takes the next element and inserts it into the sorted partition. Selection Sort's sorted elements are in their FINAL positions; Insertion Sort's sorted elements may shift later.

common_mistakes.title: Assuming it is stable
common_mistakes.explanation: The long-distance swap skips over equal elements. [3A, 3B, 1] becomes [1, 3B, 3A], reversing the order of equal elements.

recall_questions.q: What makes Selection Sort uniquely suited for write-constrained hardware?
recall_questions.answer: It performs at most N-1 swaps, the mathematical minimum for a comparison-based sort, trading cheap reads for minimal writes.
It has no mechanism to detect pre-existing order. It always scans the entire unsorted partition for the minimum, regardless of input arrangement.

recall_questions.q: Why is Selection Sort unstable? Give an example.
recall_questions.answer: The long-distance swap can vault over equal elements. [3A, 3B, 1] -> swap(3A, 1) -> [1, 3B, 3A]. The 3s are now in reversed order.

oa_questions.question: Implement Selection Sort.
oa_questions.answer: For each i from 0 to N-2: set min_index = i. For each j from i+1 to N-1: if arr[j] < arr[min_index], update min_index. After the inner loop, swap arr[i] with arr[min_index].

oa_questions.question: Find the K smallest elements in an unsorted array without fully sorting it.
oa_questions.answer: Run K passes of Selection Sort. After K passes, the first K elements of the array are the K smallest, fully sorted. Time: O(K*N).

practice.title: Sort an Array
practice.why: Implementing Selection Sort here will TLE, reinforcing why O(N^2) non-adaptive sorts fail at scale.

interesting_facts: Selection Sort is the basis for the Heap Sort algorithm. By replacing the linear scan for the minimum with a min-heap extraction (O(log N)), you get an O(N log N) sort with the same minimal-swap philosophy.
